schools.summary: Gill: some Jewish writers connect this with the foregoing law of the suspected wife — since wine leads to adultery, the Nazarite's abstinence, the forbearance of trimming the hair, and closer devotion to God were means of preserving from unchastity; women were concerned in it as well as men.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the law of the Nazarite after the law of jealousy
consecration the path of those who draw near to God

schools.summary: Gill: the Jewish canon that Gentiles have no Nazariteship, the law binding only the children of Israel, though Gentiles might bring vows and freewill offerings.
Keil & Delitzsch: when either man or woman shall separate themselves to vow a vow of a Nazarite, to separate themselves unto the LORD.

themes: When either man or woman shall separate themselves to vow a vow of a Nazarite
a freewill separation unto the LORD
a higher consecration for the willing

schools.summary: Gill: not from excess only but from drinking wine at all, that he might be more free and fit for the service of God; Aben Ezra: many transgressions are occasioned by wine, which intoxicates the mind, unfits for religious duties, and excites lust.
Clarke: the Nazarite consecrated himself in a special and extraordinary manner to God, abstaining from all the produce of the vine.

themes: He shall separate himself from wine and strong drink
not from excess only but from wine at all
a good thing renounced for a closer walk with God

schools.summary: Gill: it covered the leaves, branches, and fruit of the vine in any food, liquor, or sauce; the Jews held the unspecified vow to last thirty days at least.
Keil & Delitzsch: all the days of his separation he was to eat nothing made of the vine, from the kernels to the husk.

themes: he shall eat nothing that is made of the vine tree, from the kernels even to the husk
the whole vine forbidden
consecration a complete renunciation

schools.summary: Gill: the vow might be for a week, a month, or more, but not less than thirty days; no razor came upon his head.
Clarke: the Nazarite consecrated himself to God for a season, letting the hair grow to a full growth to be burned in the fire under the peace offering (v.18).

themes: there shall no razor come upon his head... let the locks of the hair... grow
the visible badge of the vow
a crown of consecration

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase 'all the days that he separateth himself unto the LORD' is repeated at each new branch of the law, showing each part strictly to be kept.
Keil & Delitzsch: because he wore the diadem of his God on his head in the growth of his hair and was holy to the LORD, he was to approach no dead person during his consecration.

themes: he shall come at no dead body
the diadem of consecration on his head
holiness and death not to meet on his person

schools.summary: Gill: what even the common priests might do — mourn and tend their dead kin — the Nazarite might not, being on a level with the high priest, which shows how sacred these persons were; this instructs spiritual Nazarites to abstain from what would defile, holding nothing above their consecration.

themes: He shall not make himself unclean for his father, or for his mother
because the consecration of his God is upon his head
God's claim above the dearest natural ties

schools.summary: Gill: the Nazarite was set apart for God's service, separate from all others and especially the dead, bound to abstain from wine, shaving, and defilement, and to be employed in holy and religious exercises all the days his vow was upon him.

themes: All the days of his separation he is holy unto the LORD
the abstinences serving consecration
the days a drawing near to God

schools.summary: Gill: the two words rendered 'very suddenly' are taken by some as synonymous and intensive, by others (the Targums) as a death by ignorance or chance, not wilful; the defiled Nazarite shaved his head on the seventh day, the day of his cleansing.

themes: if any man die very suddenly by him... he hath defiled the head of his consecration
shave his head... on the seventh day
the consecration broken by the least touch of death

schools.summary: Gill: this was the offering of the poorer sort, as of childbearing women and profluvious persons; the defilement could be purged only by sacrifice, typical of the sacrifice of Christ, by which sin — even the sin of holy things — is put away.
Keil & Delitzsch: on the eighth day he brought two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the door of the tabernacle.

themes: on the eighth day he shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons
the defilement purged by sacrifice
Christ cleansing the sins of holy things (1 Pet. 1:19)

schools.summary: Gill: both were necessary — the one to expiate sin, the other a gift to God in thankfulness for the acceptance of the first.
Ellicott: 'he sinned by the dead' is by reason of, or on account of, the dead, the contact reckoned a sin to be atoned.

themes: the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering
make an atonement for him... shall hallow his head
the defiled cleansed and set apart to God anew

schools.summary: Gill: he was to begin his account again from the shaving of his head, devoting as many days to the LORD as he had vowed before.
Wesley: 'lost' is, in the Hebrew, 'fall to the ground,' that is, be void or of none effect; as many days as he had before vowed.

themes: he shall consecrate unto the LORD the days of his separation... the days that were before shall be lost
a broken consecration begun afresh
holiness recovered wholly, not patched

schools.summary: Gill: this respects both what goes before (the abstinences and the case of defilement) and what follows (the offerings at the completion of the vow).
Keil & Delitzsch: this is the law for the close of the vow, when the days of separation are fulfilled, the Nazarite brought to the door of the tabernacle.

themes: this is the law of the Nazarite, when the days of his separation are fulfilled
brought unto the door of the tabernacle
the vow concluded as public worship and thanksgiving

schools.summary: Gill: the Nazarite presented his offering at the door of the tabernacle to the priest.
Keil & Delitzsch: one he lamb for a burnt offering, one ewe lamb for a sin offering, and one ram for peace offerings, each without blemish.

themes: one he lamb... for a burnt offering... one ewe lamb... for a sin offering... one ram... for peace offerings
the consecrated still needing atonement
all pointing to the unblemished Lamb

schools.summary: Gill: the unleavened bread, as at the consecration of Aaron and his sons (Lev. 8:26), though leavened bread was offered for thanksgiving peace offerings.
Ellicott and Wesley: the meal offering and drink offerings of fine flour, oil, and wine that generally accompanied the sacrifices (Num. 15:3).

themes: a basket of unleavened bread, cakes of fine flour mingled with oil
their meat offering, and their drink offerings
the oil breathing of the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the priest brought all the offerings to the altar of burnt offering and presented them to the LORD in the name of the Nazarite, the sin offering offered first, then the burnt offering.

themes: the priest shall bring them before the LORD, and shall offer his sin offering, and his burnt offering
offered in the Nazarite's name
worship accepted through a mediator

schools.summary: Gill: the ram was offered for a peace offering after the other two.
Keil & Delitzsch: the completion of the consecration vow was concentrated in the ram and the basket of unleavened bread for the peace offering, with its meal and drink offerings.

themes: he shall offer the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings... with the basket of unleavened bread
the climax of the consecration
ending in communion with God

schools.summary: Gill: this was done publicly at the door, that all might know the Nazariteship was at an end; the hair, consecrated to the Lord by the vow, was burned upon the altar fire.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Nazarite shaved the head of his separation at the door and put the hair in the fire under the peace offering.

themes: the Nazarite shall shave the head of his separation... and put it in the fire
done publicly at the door
the badge of devotion itself offered up to God

schools.summary: Gill: this was the left shoulder, the right being the priest's by another law, so the priest here had both — a peculiar honour marking the sacredness of the Nazarite's vow.
Keil & Delitzsch: the priest took the sodden shoulder of the ram, an unleavened cake and wafer, and put them on the Nazarite's hands after the hair was shaven.

themes: the priest shall take the sodden shoulder of the ram... and put them upon the hands of the Nazarite
the priest's double portion
the completed devotion presented to God

schools.summary: Gill: the priest put his hands under the Nazarite's and waved them to and fro, testifying God's goodness, his sovereign dominion, and that all the Nazarite had and did was from him and through his help; then the Nazarite might drink wine.
Keil & Delitzsch: the priest waved the portions for a wave offering before the LORD, and after that the Nazarite might drink wine, the vow discharged.

themes: the priest shall wave them for a wave offering before the LORD... the Nazarite may drink wine
all owned to be from God
the vow for a season honourably ended

schools.summary: Gill: this is what the Nazarite is obliged to do when his Nazariteship is up, according to the vow he vowed.
Keil & Delitzsch: he offered his sacrificial gifts according to the state of a Nazarite he had entered, beside what his means allowed him freely to add.

themes: This is the law of the Nazarite who hath vowed... beside that that his hand shall get
according to the vow which he vowed, so he must do
the vow paid in full, generosity beyond it welcome

schools.summary: Gill: some join the law of the priestly blessing to that of the Nazarites because both were holy persons.
Keil & Delitzsch: the spiritual character of the congregation of Israel culminated in the blessing with which the priests were to bless the people.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
from the Nazarite's holiness to the blessing of all Israel
the LORD's favour put upon his people by his ministers

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron and his sons, who succeeded him in all generations, were the persons appointed publicly to bless the people of Israel.
Keil & Delitzsch: 'on this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel,' the very words of the threefold blessing appointed.

themes: Speak unto Aaron and unto his sons... On this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel
the exact words divinely given
a standing ministry of grace through the priest

schools.summary: Gill: in the threefold use of 'Jehovah' through the blessing he sees the three divine persons — Father, Son, and Spirit — each concerned in blessing the people of God; the Father with every spiritual blessing, the Son with redeeming grace, the Spirit with sanctifying grace; first 'bless,' then 'keep,' God both bestowing good and preserving his people in it.

themes: The LORD bless thee, and keep thee
the threefold Jehovah foreshadowing the Trinity
God giving every good and guarding it

schools.summary: Gill: the shining face is God causing himself, the Sun of righteousness, to arise and shine, granting his gracious presence and the light of his countenance.
Wesley: alluding to the sun shining on the earth to enlighten and warm it — 'the Lord love thee, and make thee know that he loves thee'; we cannot but be happy if we know God loves us.

themes: The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee
the Sun of righteousness shining
the felt assurance of God's love

schools.summary: Gill: the lifted countenance is God showing his face and favour, looking cheerfully on his people, declaring himself well pleased with them in Christ, restoring the joys of his salvation; the blessing ends in peace, the fruit of his favour.

themes: The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace
God's open, smiling favour
the blessing crowned with peace

schools.summary: Gill: the priests put God's name upon the people by calling them by his name and pronouncing the blessing in the name Jehovah, three times used; the LORD promises 'I will bless them' really and truly, with blessings irreversible — unless God blesses, in vain do the priests pronounce.

themes: they shall put my name upon the children of Israel; and I will bless them
the name Jehovah set thrice upon the people
the blessing resting on God himself who surely blesses
